coredata.dc:title: Application of polyacrylonitrile nanofibers decorated with magnetic carbon dots as a resonance light scattering sensor to determine famotidine
coredata.dc:creator: Maleki, Somayeh
Madrakian, Tayyebeh
Afkhami, Abbas
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel resonance light scattering (RLS) sensor was synthesized using polyacrylonitrile nanofibers decorated with magnetic carbon dots (MCDs@NFs) nanocomposite and applied for famotidine (FMD) determination. The MCDs@NFs nanocomposite was synthesized by combining electrospinning and a simple one-step hydrothermal method. Different methods were applied in order to characterize the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite such as: scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and X-ray diffraction (XRD). Light scattering properties of the synthesized nanocomposite in the presence or absence of FMD have been selected as the detection signal considering the fact that FMD addition increases the RLS intensities of the system. Thus, the prepared nanocomposite was employed as a RLS sensor to detect FMD. A linear response was observed under the optimal conditions in range of 0.15–50.0μmolL−1 with detection limit of 0.04μmolL−1. The MCDs@NFs nanocomposite was effectively capable in determining FMD in real samples and the results were close to those results obtained by reversed-phase HPLC method (RP-HPLC).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Famotidine
Resonance light scattering sensor
Magnetic carbon dots
Polyacrylonitrile nanofibers
Electrospinning
Hydrothermal method

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure of the proposed method for detection of FMD
Point of zero charge (pHPZC) of the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite
Effect of concentration of the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical structure of FMD.

The RLS spectra of (a) MCDs@NFs nanocomposite and (b) MCDs@NFs nanocomposite mixed with FMD. Conditions: The concentration of MCDs@NFs nanocomposite and FMD was 2.0mgL−1and 50.0 μmol L−1, respectively, in B–R buffer solution (pH 6.5) at sonication time 20min.
The FT–IR spectra for (a) MCD, (b) PAN nanofibers and (c) MCDs@NFs nanocomposite.
XRD pattern of (a) PAN nanofibers and (b) MCDs@NFs nanocomposite.
SEM images of (a) MCD, (b) PAN nanofibers and (c) MCDs@NFs nanocomposite.
TEM images of (a) MCDs@NFs nanocomposite and (b) the surface edge of MCDs@NFs nanocomposite.
(a) Full-scan XPS spectrum of the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite, (b) C 1s XPS spectrum of the nanocomposite and (c) O 1s XPS spectrum of the nanocomposite.
The RLS spectra of (a) PAN nanofibers, (b) MCD and (c) MCDs@NFs nanocomposite.
Point of zero charge (pHpzc) of MCDs@NFs nanocomposite.
Effect of pH on signal variations magnitude of RLS techniques. Condition: The concentration of MCDs@NFs nanocomposite and FMD was 1.0mgL−1 and 40.0μmolL−1, respectively, at sonication time 15min.

Effect of concentration of MCDs@NFs nanocomposite on signal variations magnitude of RLS techniques. Conditions: The concentration of FMD was 40.0μmolL−1, in B–R buffer solution (pH 6.5) at sonication time 15min.

Effect of sonication time on signal variations magnitude of RLS techniques. Conditions: The concentration of FMD was 40.0μmolL−1, in B–R buffer solution (pH 6.5) and concentration of MCDs@NFs nanocomposite was 2.0mgL−1.

(a) The RLS spectra of MCDs@NFs nanocomposite in the presence of different concentration of FMD. The curves a–k represents the concentrations of FMD of 0.0, 0.15, 0.3, 0.6, 1.5, 6, 15, 20, 30, 40 and 50μmolL−1, respectively. (b) A linear relationship between the ΔIRLS value and the concentration of FMD in the solution.
Fabrication procedure of MCDs@NFs nanocomposite.
Evaluation of intra-day and inter-day precision and accuracy of the proposed method.
Influence of small variation in the assay conditions on the analytical performance of proposed method (n = 3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The optimum conditions were pH 6.5, nanocomposite concentration of 2.0mgL−1 and sonication time of 20min.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Interference of some foreign substances for 20.0µmolL−1 FMD.
Determination results of FMD in human serum sample (n = 11).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Critical t value at 95% confidence level was 2.228.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination results of FMD in pharmaceutical preparations (n = 11).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Critical t value at 95% confidence level was 2.228.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the proposed system with previous measurement methods for the determination of FMD.

head.ce:title: Application of polyacrylonitrile nanofibers decorated with magnetic carbon dots as a resonance light scattering sensor to determine famotidine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Bu-Ali Sina University, Hamedan, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemistry, Bu-Ali Sina University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel resonance light scattering (RLS) sensor was synthesized using polyacrylonitrile nanofibers decorated with magnetic carbon dots (MCDs@NFs) nanocomposite and applied for famotidine (FMD) determination. The MCDs@NFs nanocomposite was synthesized by combining electrospinning and a simple one-step hydrothermal method. Different methods were applied in order to characterize the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite such as: scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and X-ray diffraction (XRD). Light scattering properties of the synthesized nanocomposite in the presence or absence of FMD have been selected as the detection signal considering the fact that FMD addition increases the RLS intensities of the system. Thus, the prepared nanocomposite was employed as a RLS sensor to detect FMD. A linear response was observed under the optimal conditions in range of 0.15–50.0μmolL−1 with detection limit of 0.04μmolL−1. The MCDs@NFs nanocomposite was effectively capable in determining FMD in real samples and the results were close to those results obtained by reversed-phase HPLC method (RP-HPLC).

ce:section.ce:para: Famotidine (FMD) is a H2-histamine receptor antagonist to reduce gastric secretion, known as an antiulcer drug which could be administrated either intravenously or orally [1,2]. Most of the available methods for analyzing famotidine, in both biological fluids and pharmaceutical preparations samples, are performed based on chromatography with different detection modes [3–9]. Elimination of plasma interferences and preconcentration in biological fluids were performed by solid-phase extraction (SPE) procedures via a reversed-phase HPLC analysis In some of these methods [3–5]. In the other HPLC methods [6,7], at first, plasma samples are extracted by diethyl ether and then ethyl acetate is used for extraction before the chromatographic separation via C18 reversed-phase column. Sample preparation is time-consuming and complex for these methods due to requiring tedious liquid–liquid or solid-phase extraction procedures. On the other hand, most of the mentioned methods spend long chromatographic elution time for analyzing FMD and are not suitable in other conditions. Zhong et al. [2] and Campanero et al. [8] presented two HPLC procedures to analyse FMD based on MS–MS detection using tandem mass spectrometry. Both of these methods are very sensitive and have low quantitation limits. However, these methods are not employed in most laboratories because of their specialty requirement and high cost [1]. Guo et al. [9] used the HPLC column switching technique for determining FMD using the direct injection of plasma samples. The method includes direct injection of plasma to an extraction column for sample clean-up followed via switching onto an analytical column. However, High reagent consumption and HPLC system with two pumps are considered as the two most important disadvantages of this method [5]. Also, famotidine was determined by spectrophotometry methods [10–14]. The reported spectrophotometric methods based on charge transfer and ion-pair complex formation reactions suffer from one or more disadvantages like less sensitive, use of expensive reagent, use of organic solvents and involvement of liquid-liquid extraction step. The method based on condensation reaction involves boiling step for 5min and also the use of organic solvent [15].

Resonance light scattering (RLS) is employed as a powerful optical technique according to elastic light-scattering [16]. Considering advantages of RLS such as high rapidness, sensitivity, simplicity, and convenience (using an ordinary spectrofluorometer), it is an interesting technique for analytical chemists and physicists [17]. Recently, some researchers applied RLS technique to determine inorganic ions [18,19], nanoparticles, proteins [20–23], nucleic acids [24], and etc.

Metal nanostructures can strongly scatter light due to the resonant plasma oscillation of conduction electrons [25]. Local field enhancement is a result of the resonant plasma oscillation. It is usable in surface-enhanced Raman scattering [26], second-harmonic generation [27], scanning near-field optical microscopy [28], and etc. These unique optical properties originate from the susceptibility and large density of free electrons of metal nanoparticles. The collective particle plasmon mode interacts strongly with optical waves. Many studies have been done on the optical properties of metal nanoparticles, including nanospheres [29–31], nanorods [32], nanoprisms and nanowires [33–35], bimetallic nanoparticles [36,37], composite nanoparticles with a core-shell structure, and nanoparticle assemblies and chains [38–40].

Magnetite (Fe3O4) is commonly used as one of the famous magnetic materials. Due to its advantages including easy preparation, biocompatible, high surface area, catalytic activity, and low toxicity, it have been applied in biotechnology and medicine with a growing trend which would make using Fe3O4 nanoparticles for the sensing application possible [41]. Iron oxide nanoparticles usually aggregate into large clusters. Hence, their specific properties related to single-domain magnetic nanostructures are lost due to anisotropic dipolar attraction. Because of electronic/ionic charges on surface of these nanoparticles, they could show reduction/oxidation reaction electrochemically which could affect their stability [42]. Besides, surface areas of nanoparticles are large. To use the nanoparticles, they should have chemical stability, uniformity in size, and well dispersion in the media. Therefore, the strengthened magnetic nanocomposites dispersion and improved surface functional groups are very significance [43]. In this regard, Carbon dots (CDs) could be used to coat them. The CDs are defined as 0D carbon-based materials that are considered as numerous oxygen-containing functional groups (–OH, –C˭O and –COOH) on their surfaces. Their high chemical stability, optical absorptivity, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and water dispersity have made the CDs to attractive materials. Easy preparation and high surface area are considered as other advantages of the CDs [44,45].

Electrospinning is an effective technique which is introduced to fabricate one-dimensional continuous polymer nano and micro -fibers [46,47]. Due to its flexible membrane properties and facile obtainment, the electrospun fibers can be used as carriers or templates to load nanosized functional components with homogeneous dispersion to find different application requirements [47,48]. Moreover, electrospun fibers have high porosity, large specific surface area and high stability [47,49]. So far, many studies have been performed to synthesize composites by combining electrospinning and hydrothermal method [47,50–53]. Although, these studies introduced reliable approaches to synthesize composites, but in some of the studies, quantum dots, other nanoparticles, and nanofibers were synthesized separately and then used to synthesize the composite in another hydrothermal step, making the synthesis steps time consuming.

This paper has used light scattering properties of the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite to determine FMD. The study reports a new method to synthesize the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite via combining electrospinning and a simple one-step hydrothermal method. In fact, in this work, carbon dots and magnetic nanoparticles were synthesized simultaneously in only one step to form MCDs and they were placed on the nanofibers at the same step. So, it was not necessary that each one to be synthesized individually and placed on nanofibers in another step. As a result, the synthesis stages are very short. Formation of MCDs within nanofibers will not only generate new nanocomposite with excellent physiochemical properties but also help to avoid the aggregation of the particles inside the sample matrix. The detection method is the RLS. The RLS intensity is measured by the synchronous scanning at λex = λem (Δλ = 0nm). The relative RLS intensity (ΔIRLS) is acquired by the difference between the evaluation system (IRLS) and the reagent blank (I0) at 456nm (ΔIRLS = IRLS –I0).

Citric acid, FeCl3·6H2O and ethylenediamine were purchased from Merck Company (Darmstadt, Germany) and Aldrich Company (St. Louis, MO, USA). Also deionized (DI) water was used throughout the experiments. Alborz Bulk Pharmaceutical Company (Kaveh Industrial zone, Saveh, Iran) provided FMD ( Fig. 1). The FMD stock solution (1.0×10−3 molL−1) was prepared in DI water. The PAN (polyacrylonitrile) with molecular weight of 150,000g/mol was used as the polymer matrix and N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) was applied as the solvent for dissolving PAN. Britton–Robinson (B–R) buffer solution (0.04molL−1) of pH 6.5 was used for pH adjustments. In case of recovery tests, FMD tablets (20.0 and 40.0mg) provided from Pursina Company and human serum samples taken from Bessat Hospital Laboratory (Hamedan, Iran) were used.

Transmission electronic microscopy (TEM, Philips-CM10-300kV) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, XL30 and Philips Netherland and JEOL-JSM-840A) were applied to characterize morphology, structure and size of the synthesized MCDs@NFs nanocomposite. Also, an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, 38066 Riva, d/G. via M. Misone, 11/D (TN) Italy) was used to determine crystal structure of the synthesized nanocomposite. An FT-IR spectrometer (PerkinElmer model Spectrum GX) using KBr pellets were employed to record the mid-infrared spectra of the synthesized nanocomposite in the 400–4000cm−1 region. Measurements of the RLS were done by a PerkinElmer (LS50B) luminescence spectrometer with slit width = 10nm, wavelength scan rate = 500nmmin−1 and a 1.0cm quartz cell. The RLS intensity was measured by the synchronous scanning at λex = λem (Δλ = 0nm). The relative RLS intensity (ΔIRLS) was acquired by measuring the difference between the evaluation system (IRLS) and the reagent blank (I0) at 456nm (ΔIRLS = IRLS – I0). A universal ultrasonic cleaner water bath with 40kHz frequency (RoHS, Korea) was used for the tests. Also pH measurements were done by a Metrohm model 713pH meters with a combined glass electrode. Chromatography was carried out in reversed-phase conditions using HPLC apparatus (Shimadzu, 10AVP) which consists of 10AD vp pumps, SPD-M10A vp detector and ODS column C18 (4.6mm × 150mm, 5µm). The mobile phase was a mixture of 0.03molL−1 disodium hydrogen phosphate buffer–acetonitrile (93:7, v/v) at a flow rate of 1.5mL/min. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a MultiLab 2000 spectrometer (Thermo electron corporation, England).

Briefly, PAN solution (8wt%) was provided by dissolving PAN powder in the solvent of DMF with stirring for 8h at 60°C [47,54]. Then, the solution was poured into a glass syringe and connected to high-voltage power supply. A high-voltage supply of 10kV was set between the anode and cathode at a distance of 15cm for preparing electrospun PAN nanofibers.

A typical one-step hydrothermal synthesis was employed. In this synthesis, citric acid (0.5g), FeCl3·6H2O (0.5g) and ethylenediamine (5mL) were dissolved into 45mL of DI water, followed by adding 0.05g electrospun PAN nanofibers. Then, the solution was transferred into the Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (100mL) and heated at 180°C for 4h. After reaching the room temperature, the prepared brown nanofiber was washed with DI water and ethanol for several times and then dried in a vacuum oven at 60◦C for 2h ( Scheme 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure of the proposed method for detection of FMD
ce:section.ce:para: To study the effect of FMD on the RLS intensity of the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite, 2.0mgL−1 of the water dispersed MCDs@NFs nanocomposite, 2.0mL of B–R buffer solution (pH 6.5), and different amounts of FMD were added into a 5.0mL volumetric flask, diluted to the mark with DI water and sonicated for 20min until the nanocomposite was fully dispersed. Synchronous scanning at λex = λem from 200nm to 800nm was used to record the RLS spectra. It was seen that the maximum wavelength of RLS spectra is visible at 456nm. The relative RLS intensity (ΔIRLS) was acquired by measuring scattering intensity of the difference between the evaluation system (IRLS) and the reagent blank (I0) at 456nm (ΔIRLS = IRLS – I0). Fig. 2 shows the RLS spectra of the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite (a) and the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite -FMD (b).

Ten FMD tablets were powdered and homogenized to pharmaceutical analysis. Then a specific amount of the homogenized powder (equal to weight of one tablet) was imported into a 25mL volumetric flask and dissolved in DI water. The flask content was sonicated (for 15min) and centrifuged (for 10min) at 4000rpm. Then, the product was filtered and a sufficient volume from it was used. Finally, the solution was analyzed according to the procedure and the ΔIRLS was measured at the maximum wavelength of RLS spectra (456nm). Besides, validity of the proposed method was also investigated via analyzing the prepared sample of FMD tablets by RP-HPLC [1,55–57].

For human serum sample preparation, various amounts of FMD were added to the 1.0mL of serum sample and then acetonitrile (0.8mL) was added to remove serum protein. In the next step, the mixture was centrifuged (for 15min) at 4000rpm. After the supernatant was transferred into a volumetric flask (5.0mL). Finally, the ΔIRLS was measured using the mentioned procedure. Moreover, human serum sample was also analyzed by RP-HPLC using the standard addition method [1,55,57]. In this regard, the same method which is described above was applied in preparing the human serum samples, and same amounts of the prepared solution were distributed among several volumetric flasks. Increasing volumes of FMD stock solution were added to each flask and after that the mixtures were analyzed. Commonly elimination of matrix effects from a measurement is done by the standard addition method [58].

Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and X-ray diffraction (XRD) were applied to characterize the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite. The FT-IR spectra ( Fig. 3) were used to explore the functional groups of PAN nanofibers, MCDs and the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite. For MCD (Fig. 3a), the peaks between 3200 and 3500cm−1 indicate the existence of O-H and N-H. The band at 1652.4cm−1 with a shoulder about 1585.6cm−1 is due to CO-NH stretching vibration and N-H bending vibration, respectively. The band of 574cm−1 is attributed to Fe-O bond. The PAN nanofibers (Fig. 3b) showed the characteristic peak at around 2937cm−1 related to the stretching vibration of the methylene (–CH2–) group. The peaks at 1454.3cm−1 and 2243.2cm−1 are due to the bending vibration of methylene (–CH2–) and the stretching vibration of nitrile groups (–CN–), respectively [59–61]. The FTIR spectrum of the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite is illustrated in Fig. 3c. The spectrum of the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite shows a band at 1551.2cm−1. Due to the bending vibration of N-H and band at 1639cm−1 is belongs to CO-NH group as peaks between 3000 and 3500cm−1 indicate the existence of O-H and N-H. The peaks at 2238.5, 2934.5 and 1445cm−1 are due to the stretching vibrations of nitrile groups (C≡N), stretching and bending vibrations of methylene groups (-CH2-), respectively. The characteristic absorption band of Fe-O in Fe3O4 (around 577cm−1) is observed in this Fig [59,60]. The XRD was performed to investigate the crystalline structures of the as-synthesized MCDs@NFs nanocomposite. Fig. 4 shows that both PAN nanofibers (a) and the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite (b) possess the same diffraction peak at 2θ = 17°, corresponding to the (200) plane of PAN. Also, the as-synthesized MCDs@NFs nanocomposite shows the diffraction peaks of fcc phase of Fe3O4 (JCPDS No. 082-1533), i.e. (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), (440) [54,59,60,62]. The morphologies of MCD, PAN nanofibers and the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite were observed by SEM and TEM characterizations and the results are shown in Fig. 5 and Fig. 6, respectively. Fig. 5a shows the SEM image of MCDs. It can be seen that MCDs have a nearly uniform distribution of the particles size. The diameter of MCDs is about 50nm. The SEM image of PAN nanofibers is indicated in Fig. 5b. It can be observed that PAN nanofibers exhibited a relatively smooth surface with the average diameter of about 450nm. The SEM image of MCDs@NFs nanocomposite indicates MCDs were successfully coated on the PAN nanofibers (Fig. 5c). The TEM image reveals the presence of uniformly MCDs on the surface of the PAN nanofibers backbone (Fig. 6a). Fig. 6b shows TEM image of the surface edge of the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite, confirming the surface layer of PAN nanofibers is composed of MCDs with an average size about 50nm. This coating of MCDs provides numerous active sites on the PAN nanofibers backbone, allowing the increase of scattering signal. Meanwhile, MCDs enhanced surface roughness, consequently increased the surface area of PAN nanofibers [54]. The XPS spectra were also employed to confirm the composition of the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite and the results are shown in Fig. 7. As shown in Fig. 7a, the characteristic signals of Fe (13.25wt%), O (31.54wt%), N (17.34wt%) and C (37.87wt%) elements are clearly observed in the product. In detail, two major peaks at 711.6 and 724.2eV are related to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively [63]. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s of the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite can be de-convoluted into four peaks at 284.1, 285.3, 286.1 and 288.2eV assigning to carbon in the form of C-C/C˭C, C-O/C-N, C˭N and C˭O, respectively (Fig. 7b). Also, Fig. 7c shows Fe-O (530.4eV), C˭O (531.7eV) and C-OH/C-O-C (532.4eV) of O 1s spectrum of the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite [63–65].

Fig. 8 illustrates the RLS spectra of PAN nanofibers, MCD and MCDs@NFs nanocomposite. The wavelength of maximum light scattering (λmax) of RLS is located at 456nm and their intensities are in the order of MCDs@NFs nanocomposite > MCD > PAN nanofibers. The average diameter of MCDs@NFs nanocomposite is ~565nm, which is much greater than 1/20 of the wavelength of the incident light. Therefore, the light scattering of the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite is the result of Mie scattering rather than Rayleigh scattering. According to the Mie scattering theory, the RLS properties have close relationships with morphology of the particles and the molecular volume. The higher the RLS intensity can be obtained in bigger molecular volume. It is important to note that small magnetic spheres magnetism (the Magnetic Mie scattering) could affect the Mie scattering. Also, the magnetization of small clusters with radius larger than 10nm is size dependent. However, the mechanism of the effect of magnetic Fe3O4 on the RLS is still unclear [17,66].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Point of zero charge (pHPZC) of the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The pHPZC of the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite was measured in 0.01molL−1 NaNO3 solution. Amount of 0.02g of the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite was added to 20.0mL of 0.01molL−1 NaNO3 in several beakers. The initial pH values were set at 3.0, 4.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0 and 9.0 for the solutions using 0.01molL−1 of NaOH and/or HNO3 solutions. After recording the initial pHs, parafilm was applied to cover the beakers and then shaken for 24h. The final pH values were recorded and the differences between the initial and final pH (ΔpH) vs. initial pH values were plotted [67,68]. The pHPZC corresponds to the pH with ΔpH = 0. The pHPZC for the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite was determined using the above procedure and was obtained as 5.5. The results are shown in Fig. 9.

Intensity variations of the signal provided by RLS technique in relation to the solution pH were evaluated in the range of 3.0–9.0 using B–R buffer solution. As can be seen in Fig. 10, the maximum value of ΔIRLS appeared in pH = 6.5 and at lower pHs a significant decrease is visible in ΔIRLS, whereas there is a gentle decrease for higher pHs. So, we selected pH 6.5 for further optimization process. After adding the FMD, formation of larger nanocomposite led to an increase in the RLS intensities of the system [66,69]. Another possible mechanism for the observed increase is the change of FMD in form at above and below of its pKa value. Considering the pKa value of FMD (pKa(FMD) = 7.1) [70,71], at pH > 7.1, the predominant form of FMD is its neutral form and at the other ranges is its protonated form. Besides, considering the calculated pHPZC, the surface charge of nanocomposite is negative at pH > 5.5 and is positive at pH < 5.5. So, electrostatic interactions may be responsible for the pH effect and consequently the observed enhancement in the RLS. Then in pH 6.5 effects of some buffer systems (such as Britton–Robinson, citrate and phosphate salt) on the ΔIRLS were studied. Based on the results, B–R buffer solution was the most efficient among mentioned buffer systems. The effect of B–R buffer volume was also investigated and the results showed that 2.0mL of this buffer can provide maximum ΔIRLS. Therefore 2.0mL of the B–R buffer solution (0.04molL−1) with pH 6.5 was selected as the optimum experimental medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of concentration of the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of MCDs@NFs nanocomposite's concentration on the RLS intensity of the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite in the absence and presence of FMD was studied. The ΔIRLS for 40.0μmolL−1 of FMD in B–R buffer solution (pH 6.5) and sonication time 15min with nanocomposite dosage of 0.1–5.0mgL−1 were obtained. As shown in Fig. 11, the ΔIRLS was increased with increasing the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite dosage to maximum concentration of 2.0mgL−1 and then stabled at higher concentrations of nanocomposite. This increase in ΔIRLS is due to availability of the surface area of the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite [66]. Therefore, the nanocomposite dosage of 2.0mgL−1 which is in range of the luminescence spectrometer intensity was chosen in the further experiments.

To study the sonication time influences on the RLS intensity of the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite in the absence and presence of FMD, RLS spectrum was recorded at different time intervals ( Fig. 12). The ΔIRLS for 40.0μmolL−1 of FMD in B–R buffer solution (pH 6.5) with nanocomposite dosage of 2.0mgL−1 and different sonication time intervals of 0–40min were obtained. The nanocomposite agglomerations could be separated by different levels using effective ultrasonication. It increases the number of scattering centers and whereby, a clear increase of scattering signals is predictable. On the other hand, increase in the amount of FMD adsorption as a result of the availability of higher surface area of the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite also causes an increase in the signal [66,67]. Results showed that the ΔIRLS was increased initially and after 20min of sonication it showed a stable trend. Therefore, RLS spectrum was recorded at sonication time of 20min.
The proposed method was validated for linearity, sensitivity, precision, accuracy, robustness and selectivity.

Under the optimal conditions, the RLS spectra of the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite with different amounts of FMD were recorded. As illustrated in Fig. 13(a), the addition of a series of increasing concentrations of FMD resulted in the gradual enhancement of the RLS intensity of the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite system. As shown in Fig. 13(b), there was a good linear relationship (Eq. (1)) between the ΔIRLS and FMD concentration in the range of 0.15–50.0μmolL−1.

Sensitivity parameters such as the limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were calculated using the following equations:
(2)
L O D = 3
S
b
/ m
(3)
L O Q = 10
S
b
/ m
where m and Sb are the slope of the calibration graph and standard deviation of eight reagent blank determinations, respectively. The LOD and LOQ were found to be 0.04µmolL−1 and 0.13µmolL−1 respectively, which show the excellent sensitivity of the proposed method.

Intra-day precision and accuracy of the proposed method were assessed by replicate analysis (n = 7) of two different concentrations of the FMD on a same day. Inter-day precision and accuracy were determined by testing the same concentrations of the FMD during seven consecutive days. The analytical results are presented in Table 1. The values of the relative standard deviation (RSD%) and relative error (RE%) confirm good precision and accuracy of the proposed method.

Robustness was studied by investigating the influence of small variation in different experimental variables including pH, concentration of the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite, and sonication time on the analytical performance of the method. In these experiments, one experimental parameter was changed while the other parameters were kept constant, and the recovery percentage was calculated ( Table 2). It was revealed that the small variations in each of the variables had no significant effect on the results, indicating that the proposed method is reliable.

For investigating the selectivity of the proposed technique for determination of FMD, the response of technique for several kinds of ions and biomolecules in optimum condition and concentration of 20.0μmolL−1 of FMD was obtained. Since tablet and human serum are real samples which have been used in this study, we mostly investigated the compounds that may be as interference in the real samples. The results are presented in Table 3. As can be seen from the table, the common ions such as Na+, K+, Cl− and CO3 2- did not show interference with FMD detection. It was observed that the common interference in biological samples in determining FMD including fructose, urea, starch, ascorbic acid, sucrose, glucose, tyrosine, cysteine and citric acid did not have effective interference. Also investigating the effects of interference uric acid and acetaminophen showed that there is no interference with the system to the ratio of 30 times. On the other hand, since all the drugs are not consumed simultaneously, significant interference in the determination system is not created. Other side, determination of FMD in pH and other optimized parameters increase the system selectivity. Thus, the proposed detection system has good selectivity for the determination of FMD in biological and pharmaceutical samples.

The accuracy and validity of the proposed method was further determined by performing recovery studies. At first, the proposed method in real sample analysis was examined in human serum samples. The blood tissue species are separated after serum sample preparation (see Section 2.6). Based on the results presented in Table 4, the human serum sample after testing was free from FMD and no increase in scattering intensity of nanocomposite was observed, therefore, different concentrations of this drug were spiked to the human serum sample. The signals were determined and then the recovery values were calculated, the summarized results are presented in Table 4. The recovery of the spiked samples ranged between 94.40% and 101.06%. In the next stage, FMD tablets 20.0 and 40.0mg were used. According to Table 5, the results were close together for the pharmaceutical preparation samples of FMD and the values declared on the labels. The recovery values were 98.77% and 103.05%, which were satisfactory. Further, the detected values were also compared with RP-HPLC. The results were reported in Tables 4 and 5. The obtained results using the proposed method and RP-HPLC were in good agreement. These results demonstrated the ability of MCDs@NFs nanocomposite for determination of FMD in both of the human serum and pharmaceutical samples.

The performance of the proposed method has been also compared with other analytical systems for determination of FMD. The results are shown in Table 6. It was revealed that the mentioned method is economical, simple, and rapid compare to some other methods. Also, this method has wide linear range and the detection limit is better than some other methods. It should be noted that some researchers [1,5,8] used chromatographic methods to determine FMD which have high degree of sensitivity. Whereas, the methods require large amount of high purity organic solvents, generation of high waste amount, require complex and expensive equipment, labour-intensive sample preparation procedure and personnel skilled in chromatographic techniques [15,72]. On the other hand, the reliability and precision of the results by polarography depend on the capillary characteristics which are often not reproducible [11]. Hence, these disadvantages could limit use of the method in determining FMD. It is reported that capillary electrophoresis (CE) method has poor sensitivity in result of the short optical path and the small sample injection volume, whereas the method presented in this study can determine FMD with good sensitivity [73–75]. Also, the proposed method in this study has significant advantages in compared to spectrophotometric methods which have long sample pretreatment, undefined reaction time and suffers from poor precision and poor sensitivity [10,15,76,77].

In this study, the MCDs@NFs nanocomposite was synthesized with aim of FMD determination. Light scattering properties of the synthesized nanocomposite in presence or absence of the FMD were selected as the detection signal because the FMD increased the RLS intensities of the system. Various factors that can potentially affect scattering efficiency were optimized. The results showed that there is a good linear relationship between the ΔIRLS and the concentration of FMD in optimum conditions. The RLS sensor showed a low detection limit and wide linear range in detecting FMD in compare to some other methods. Furthermore, the proposed nanocomposite could act as RLS sensor for FMD detection in pharmaceutical preparations and human serum samples. A comparison between the results of the proposed method and RP-HPLC indicates that the method is sensitive and the results are comparable.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the Bu-Ali Sina University Research Council and Center of Excellence in Development of Environmentally Friendly Methods for Chemical Synthesis (CEDEFMCS) for providing support to this work.
